does: This paper develops inference-time alignment techniques that modify model behavior during generation without retraining. The core contribution is SLOP (sharpened logarithmic opinion pool), which combines multiple generative reward models through a temperature-adjusted ensemble approach. The method introduces a calibration algorithm for weighting ensemble components to combat reward hacking—where models exploit loopholes in reward specifications rather than achieving intended behaviors. The authors demonstrate experimentally that SLOP-based alignment maintains alignment quality while improving robustness against reward specification gaming, offering a lightweight alternative to reinforcement learning that can adapt as reward targets and alignment objectives change.
connects: This work builds on recent inference-time alignment literature that frames alignment as sampling from reward-tilted distributions, extending earlier theoretical justifications for these methods. It combines ideas from ensemble learning and logarithmic opinion pools with modern reward modeling approaches used in large language model alignment. The paper responds to the growing recognition that reinforcement learning-based alignment is expensive and inflexible, while reward hacking remains a persistent problem in deployed systems. The use of temperature scaling and ensemble diversity connects to broader work on calibration and uncertainty quantification in machine learning, particularly in managing model confidence and combining multiple learners.
enables: This approach opens directions for more efficient alignment practices by enabling continual updates to reward models without model retraining, important as objectives evolve or new failure modes emerge. The framework could facilitate research on understanding and mitigating reward hacking by providing interpretable mechanisms to diagnose which ensemble components enable exploitative behaviors. Future work might extend SLOP to heterogeneous reward models, explore dynamic weight adjustment during deployment, or apply the framework to multi-objective alignment where different reward models capture distinct values. The lightweight nature of inference-time methods could enable real-time alignment adaptation and support safety-critical applications requiring rapid response to newly discovered vulnerabilities.
